commentBody: My first under 15:00 Sunday after years of daily solving! 14:34.

Great to see you here again, David! 
I gather your girlfriend is here as your PLUS ONE.

How nice to see a puzzle from David Steinberg, and one with such a clever theme. I especially like MISSED A BEAU and CONTACT LEOS, and I love how the changed letters spell out PLUS ONE. 

Today I learned about GUANACOs. 

Thanks, David. That was fun. 

So, I guess the original puzzle title derives from an entreaty to Mr. Gingrich ….

NEWT, PLEASE

Spotted the trick in a number of entries (54 and 94 across). For 51 across, I initially used the T not the U! Still needed the column to understand what was happening.

Somehow, somewhere I knew the phrase “GRODY to the max”?!!?

Thank you
Bummed that I solved the puzzle without noticing the cleverness. I need new glasses: Using my phone to solve I didn’t even see the circles. 🙄
My weekend was full of DISSONANTCHORES, so I'll drink to that!  The trick became clear after CONTROLLEDCHAPS, the circles no doubt gave it away easily.  As always, was an enjoyable solve.

Gut course at the funeral director’s training?

CASKET WEAVING

State the bedroom is in after the teenager races off to school?

COULDN’T CARE MESS

Home movie viewer belting along with the 2001 soundtrack?

ALTO SPRACH ZARATHUSTRA
It's 7:49 pm on a Saturday and I'm only seeing 7 comments? Am I in upside-down-land?

Didn't even notice the circles ... but the crosses didn't rely on the trick, so I don't see the point -- I just thought the answers were clever. I did solve it a lot faster than I usually do on the Sunday puzzle, though.

[Magic in the grid, as created today by David Steinberg]




BO(X) WOW

Is this the David Steinberg that draws the Far Side cartoons?

(Asking for a friend. I forget his name right now.)

For me this was the easiest Sunday ever, but very welcomed after Saturday dumpster fire!

Rating: 
8/10
Once again, Star Trek does me in:  Teri for Jeri.

Truly wish the iOS version of the NYTimes Games app worked correctly. 
The ‘Congratulations’ screen cannot be dismissed by clicking the ‘X’ in upper right corner so that I may admire my completed puzzle. 
There have been 2 updates to the app since this issue arose. 
Have messaged support, but crickets. 
Aside from a ‘force quit’ the best workaround I have found requires toggling ‘Display Zoom’ to ‘Default’. This is found under Settings/Display and Brightness.
Is this the David Steinberg that is a big fan of Gary Larson?

First and foremost, welcome back to our neck of the woods, David! What a lovely surprise. I started the solve with a smile when I saw the byline, knowing that the puzzle would be creative, intelligent, and elegant. I ended the solve with a smile because David, as usual, delivered on all three counts. And throughout the solve I also smiled, but especially when the clue for RAIN showed up as [Common forecast for Portland]. LOL, you betcha! Right now though, it so happens to be a gorgeous, blue-skied, 70°F Spring late afternoon. That means that half the local population is elated, basking in some outdoor activity in the sun, having survived another gray winter singing along to Seal's version of "I Can't Stand the Rain" - https://youtu.be/BRIp0JCM2_8?si=uAV0El-UNGqUzfYb The other half is in hiding at home, curtains drawn, or a dark coffee shop, alarmed by whatever that absurdly bright thing is up in the sky, singing along to Garbage's "I'm Only Happy When It Rains" - https://youtu.be/GpBFOJ3R0M4?si=JuNMEeulKcTcxlvc Me, I tread the middle ground, outdoors but in the shade, where I got to enjoy this most excellent puzzle that showcases so well the refined subtlety with which David executes his conceits. Thank you so much! Rain or shine, David, your puzzles are always five stars.

F-SHARP or [G-flat]? (Which could almost be a theme entry, come to think of it.) Well, Bach preferred F-sharp, probably because he was really thinking of F major, and just sticking a sharp in front of every note. But the moody, melancholy romantics preferred G-flat--a trend which started with Schubert: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VM7LFeDwKiM&list=RDVM7LFeDwKiM&start_radio=1 Except when they didn't: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TDGHR4lj3yc&list=RDTDGHR4lj3yc&start_radio=1 TRUE STORY: This afternoon, biking home after work, I rode through Detroit's Eastern (farmer's) Market. I was hoping to find some rhubarb. It was raining--not drizzling, raining. I was soaked. Between two of the stalls was a young musician, seated at an electric piano, which he had carefully wrapped up in blankets to protect it from the rain. He was playing Beethoven's Moonlight Sonata, from memory. Quite well. I went up and spoke to him: "You know, every day I do the New York Time crossword puzzle, and yesterday, one long entry worked out to MOONLIGHT SONATA." "Really?" he replied, "how was it clued?" "Really a deep dive, in my opinion--clued as 'piece which was the inspiration for Chopin's Fantasie-Impromptu,' or something like that." "You mean this?" and immediately launched into the opening licks of the Chopin. I stuffed his tip jar. But none of the vendors had any rhubarb left.
Great fun to do a David Steinberg puzzle again. Thanks so much, it was a 💥 I smiled through all the themes.
The clue about dishes was a little obscure, but I enjoyed the rest of the puzzle.
Good themes, but this one seemed to play to my strengths. Finished in 18 minutes, 13 under average. And great to see David back on a Sunday.

“—Lang Syne”? "Richard of 'Pretty Woman’” ? I know what happened. Someone at the Times confused the Sunday crossword puzzle with the Monday puzzle. It’s understandable. New Yorkers leave the office early on Friday on Memorial Day weekend. 

Many folks I know finished in record time, but no one felt good about it.
The link to the constructors first puzzle doesn't seem to be working 😕

It’s been awhile since we’ve seen you David—welcome back.
Really my favorite type of puzzle. I hope people checked out the title because it helped (even though there was a revealer of sorts ultimately).
But a fun punny theme, one that was enjoyable even after you figured it out, and actually helped the solve (but didn’t give it away). DISSONANT CHORES was the only one that didn’t strike quite the right note for me, but the rest were fabulous!
Reasonable challenge in the rest of the puzzle, with clever clues and some great accessory fill—LIPREADERS, EMOJIPEDIA.
And JERI  Ryan. Note to future constructors—You have my permission to remind me of Jeri Ryan every puzzle, every day. I promise I will never complain and it will never get old.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Squish two Tuesdays together and Presto Change-o...Sunday.

Between my eyesight and the bigger grid (meaning smaller squares), I couldn't see the circles, which also meant I didn't figure out the theme as easily. I noticed the okay/OLAY thing, and mail/NAIL, but a part of my brain wanted them all to be the same letter. And I didn't see the added word that the changed letters made. 

...it was a good puzzle but I'm just grumpy tonight.
One of the rare Sunday puzzles I could actually complete.
splendid theme

I should really go back to skipping Sunday crossword puzzles at the NYT. Easy ones like today's are just boring - it feels relaxing to do a fast, straightforward grid on Monday, but an equally unchallenging Sunday just isn't doing it for me - typing in 130 mostly obvious answers brings neither joy nor satisfaction. Conversely, hard Sundays just tire me out, and they are also a nightmare to flyspeck if guesses were involved and I didn't get a gold start.

I used to eschew Sundays and it felt great. I guess I have this naive hope that some Sunday will finally be the one I'll enjoy - but experience appears to prove it's just a pipe dream.

Hopefully I'll remember my own advice next week and spare myself another meh moment.

The photo accompanying the Wordplay column shows a technique of making beautiful "paintings" out of sand art. The monks create these masterpieces with such care and precision. Here is a video that shows their unbelievable skill andmastery: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WBrYUlOYK0U (It reminds me of some of my paint-by-numbers painting efforts, with tiny little areas to color in different colors!) But then the sand art is blown away and destroyed--I guess to remind us of the impermanence of life. This reminds me of the story of Penelope, wife of Odysseus. When he was gone for many long years, other suitors approached Penelope to wed a new ruler. She only wanted to wait for Odysseus and promised them that when she finished weaving the tapestry she was working on, she would choose one of them. So every day Penelope worked on her tapestry, and every night when no one could see, she unraveled it, sacrificing her sleep.

Our stoical British guys here on our annual trip to Honduras are not so stoic...   In fact, our CHAPS are unCONTROLLED to the max!! 

It's the first puzzle I have had a chance to do in a few days and somehow, despite feeling quite crazy and exhausted in our circumstances, I finished in record time. Go figure!

In short, I dun did this puzz.
Emojipedia?  Gag me.
69D/88A stalled me. Before we had EMOJIs, we had EMOTIs, which were ASCII character faces :) Without knowing 88A, TERI or JERI can fit that crossing

I like doing the crosswords and Spelling Bee (although I only do the pangrams) except for one thing: getting that message that says the crossword puzzle is filled up but something's wrong. Nobody needs that message; you already know your last letter is wrong. When I get that last letter wrong it's always because the answers come from two things I know nothing about, like soap actors' names or some thing on the web I never imagined existed, and I'm reduced to guessing to finish up. Then, with each wrong guess that damn message comes again, over and over. Really infuriating. It's the NYT software going nyah, nyah at me. Any living person who stood there and did that to you while you're racing the clock would be risking a bloody nose, so why do you NYT people make it do that when it's annoying as hell. Just as one is forced to conclude those six Republican Supremes have killed the Voting Rights Act because they want to help Republicans get all the leverage white people had in pre-VRA Dixie so Republicans can get and keep power there permanently, I'm also forced to conclude some jerk at the NYT wanted to make that end-of-puzzle experience irritating and unpleasant  -- and succeeded.

There are four species of South American camelids, all of them utterly gorgeous animals. Llamas and alpacas are commonly domesticated, whereas guanacos and vicuñas are wild. That's them in order of size as well.
I always like noticing a little Peruvian reference, thanks David!
Feel like I might have been the only one to put in LaPdancERS before LIPREADERS...
Word of the day, grody 😜

Theme, movie edition:

[Many monks]
[The world of audio paparazzi]
[Paul Newman character gives his buddy’s hat a try]
[From tilling to tofu!]
[Look]




HOODFELLAS  
SOME MIKE IT HOT
BUTCH CASSIDY AND THE SUNDANCE LID
SOY STORY
DO THE SIGHT THING

It's nice to see David Steinberg back.  While not a particularly challenging puzzle, the theme entries were fun to figure out, so I focused on trying to get them without too many crosses.  DISSONANT CHORES was probably my favorite.  The easiest for this August baby was CONTACT LEOS.

So, dating to get access to a NYT subscription?  That's new to me, but I get it.  My now husband and I were happily together for many years before a change in health insurance status pushed us to play the game and get officially married.  Yes, non-U.S. solvers, that's actually a thing here!

Good Sunday solve! Enjoyed getting the theme answers enough, that I missed the trick until reading Wordplay. Got to go back to get the aha pleasure after. And that was ok for me. Liked learning the term emojipedia.

Oh, sweet to see David back after more than a year.  Some marquee constructors specialize in themeless puzzles, and others in themed, but David does it all –he’s done 60 themeless puzzles and 51 themed in the Times, marked by wit and skill.

I loved the trip [White bed, perhaps] took me through, where first I flipped through a mental montage of beds with white frames and covers, then confidently slapped SNOW in, but then it didn’t work; eventually, with a lovely aha, RICE became clear, and I inwardly high-fived David -- got my money's worth right there.  

It was fun to guess at the theme answers with as few fill-ins as possible, and it was impressive that he found theme answers that not only were of the specific letter lengths to fit symmetry, but the trick letters also spelled PLUS ONE – in order.  Wow!

I always feel like I’m in good hands when doing David’s puzzles, and afterward, that I coursed through high quality.  Thank you for what you do, David, and for what you did today!
Boring and easy for a Sunday
Normally a big fan of Steinberg puzzles. This one had so many short answers I got bored. Maybe a little more humor in the cluing would have helped.
Thanks to David Steinberg for archiving!I’ve been going backwards working only on Friday/Saturdays, halfway through 2016, so quite aways to go . . .

The face I made for  [They twist beneath the surface] doesn’t yet appear at Emojipedia.


Too bad you can’t go through the entire alphabet for each circled square. Otherwise, a fun Sunday puzzle.

Meh. I was going to leave that as my one-word comment of the day, but I hate it when other do that, so a brief explanation. Too many uninspTired three- and four-letter words. A theme that was not terribly clever and produced not terribly amusing themers. Nothing to get excited about. A general lack of "INSPO". TIL that there was such a thing as EMOJIPEDIA, which I would have preferred to remain ignorant about. [Feel free to comment with the "prefer to remain ignorant" emoji.] My favorite part of today's puzzle: The Buddhist mandala illustrating the answer SAND ART. A nice reminder that Beauty can come from the simplest elements and that everything in this world is ephemeral. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WBrYUlOYK0U "Let it go. This, too, shall pass."
I don’t care if it was too easy - I completed a Sunday in THIRTEEN MINUTES today. This high is gonna keep my afloat all week long!

Actually liked this one, which for me is a Sunday rarity.

Before being altered, each of the thematic answers really was something well known. And the clues for the wacky altered versions were all pretty good---though "Loading the dishes and unloading the dishes, e.g.?" still isn't evoking DISSONANTCHORES for me. Will need to ponder that one a bit more.

Having the circled entries spell PLUS ONE was a nice bonus.

My only complaint was that the puzzle on the whole seemed a bit too simple. Was done in less than 30 minutes, which is fast for me on a Sunday. Then again, I got the theme fairly quickly, and having it certainly helped me fill the grid.

Well done, Mr. Steinberg!

Well, cute theme but a tough one for me of course. Even beyond the theme answers there were eight down answers appearing for the first time. But... finally tumbling to the trick was a big turning point and that's always a nice touch. And of course a puzzle find today. A Wednesday from February 5, 2014 by Tracy Gray. Four theme answers and I'll highlight the letters related to the trick: hellorhigHWATEr THEWAltons grEATWHiteshark soWHATElseisnew And the reveal: "Breakfast cereal, or a hint to what's found in the answers to the four starred clues." SHREDDEDWHEAT Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/5/2014&g=59&d=A See you tomorrow. ...

Polling the Commentariat on their preferred pronunciation of ESCHEW.

ESCHEW is a word that an educated person ought to know [IMHO] and be able to use in writing.  But to say it out loud almost always seems pretentious.
And so, one seldom hears it.

Do you prefer "ess-CHOO" or "e-SHOO"?
Or do you have some other pronunciation?
I laughed out loud at 37A. What a puzzle!

Got through the puzzle after a slow start and finished with a minimum of look ups, but didn't get the "win".  Had a couple of entries that fit, but were incorrect, DIBSONTHECHORES (instead of DISSONANTCHORES) being the one causing a few crossings to be wrong.  The connections on the theme answers was too nuanced for me.

28:31 faster than average. 

This means one of two things:
1.  This puzzle was really easy
2.  My average times are horrifyingly long

Or most likely, both.
Great puzzle, greater name.

Just focused on the theme clues and that was fun trying to guess the riddle clues.  Did look for a reveal but missing that tried to make something out of the alternate letters - OKTRNMD must be an acronym for something.  

es shoe?

I'm in the camp of having enjoyed this puzzle, despite the relatively easy fill of shorter words once I got a couple of the themed entries. I'm pretty sure it was CONTACTLEOS that was the key to the dominoes falling in the SEC and that got me "clued" in for the rest. (NWC was the last to register and complete). And it's pretty impressive to have made this many lengthy themed entries work with the added trick of PLUSONE. Yes, some of the short word clues could have been made more difficult, but I have plenty to do today and my step son is coming to visit from out of state, so I'm okay that it didn't take me a lot longer to solve. (Then why, you ask, am I spending time browsing and responding to Comments. Because I did enjoy this one and was curious to see if I could fairly accurately predict the breakdown between "like this one, clever, fun" versus the "too easy, boring, waste of my time" comments. Answer -- yes.)

I don’t get the entry about dishwasher chores, but otherwise, what a great theme!

It’s one of those themes that had absolutely zero effect on the solving process, and didn’t even click for me until a post-solve review…but now that I’ve grokked it, I absolutely love it, circles and all.

Anyone else go back and solve the June 16, 2011, puzzle? Wow, that was tough for me. But fun. The link in Puzzle Info to Wordplay didn't work, and it took me some time to figure out the theme. Diabolical! Frankly, looking at the completed puzzle, I'm surprised that I solved it. Lot of room for unknown errors.

If any of us were wondering what he's been up to since finishing HS and university, this may be a partial answer....
This took me a good while (the coffee was late; then it got cold...) what with MOLE MAN and McDAVID and  YOGA BALLs....
The only truly painful, really cruel entry was EMOJIPEDIA, but eventually I GOT 'er DONE.  

I question a couple of spellings, such as TOOTSY vs IE.... and then we have GRODY vs GRODDY (neither of which deserves to be A Word.)  
Balanced against SEVEN Themers, though, one must concede that this is quite an achievement.

My favorite answer --> dissonant chords. I love playing them, especially the SUS2 and SUS4s. They create great tension, and the musician chooses if and when they resolve. A well-placed dissonant as an opening or final chord sounds so sinister. Think... opening chord in Hard Day's Nig

...
My eyeballs are sore from all the rolling.

Heat wave here - it's 29C, and 32C (over 89F) tomorrow. Hotter than many midsummers. 

Oh dear, I missed yet another clue for TSA. But "may request"? We all have to take off jackets and coats. 

I ambled through this one in about 50 minutes. I found getting the theme clues the hardest, I had to get most of the crossers. I  didn't realise there was a theme, and forgot to check the circles. 

I looked up and found out about 'pink slips'. A long time ago, we used to 'get the sack' ie get your sack of tools and go, a term still used today. Then it was 'get your cards' - employers paid into the National Insurance scheme (welfare) by buying and sticking a stamp on a card for you every week. When you left you took these cards with you. In Scotland we said 'get your jotters'. 

Nice for a this hot Sunday.
Solved in 20:28! My best Sunday :)

At first we weren’t loving this theme, especially because we couldn’t figure out what a controlled chaows was. Totally forgot that CHAOS is a word. Some of our favorite answers were EMOJIPEDIA, CURIO, TOOTSY, and the clue “Awl, for one.” Another beautiful Sunday in East Aurora. You guys have to check out the strawberry rhubarb danish at Elm St Bakery! Delightfully yours, Milly B and Cam K

I usually have to look at the answer key for majority of the answers. This time, however, most of them I didn’t. At first I peeked in on the themed answers and tricky clues. Most I was able to fill in on my own before needing to look at the key near the end of the solve. This was possibly one of the most enjoyable puzzles I’ve solved. (Incidentally, I think it’s nice that the writer of today’s puzzle shares his subscription with his girlfriend for the Mini. What a sweet guy!)
Lots of fun David -- well done. I had to fly speck for ages cuz I thought EMOTIPEDIA sounded as good as anything. Teri, Jeri, finally got it right. Whew...

MOLE MAN was kind of a joke, as supervillains go. He discovered a cache of ancient technology in a cave, and figured out how to use it in his obsessive desire to wipe out the surface world that had rejected him. Sort of a poor man's Doctor Doom.

I'm in a bad mood, as NBC are showing two Premier Leagues matches, and neither one is Arsenal, which is streaming only. C'mon man, they're lifting the trophy today!

A crossword that was both fun AND clever, I found it immensely enjoyable. 

I had mistakenly typed in "Teri" instead of JERI, and EMOtIPEDIA looked correct to me, so it took me a lot of fly-specking to find my mistake.

As a Star Trek fan, I am appalled by this error!
I didn't like "Dissonant Chores" at all. I understand about "dissonant chords," but loading and unloading the dishwasher doesn't have even any "dissonant" tones about it....
Can someone explain the theme of the midi puzzle? Thank you.

👍👍👍👍👍👍👍

Thanks!
Fun and breezy. A nice change of pace compared to yesterday's brutal fill.
Liked most of it, wasn't a huge fan of how the bottom came together, specifically "taker"

I started off my solve by typing "stab" for the first answer. Then typed SLAB for the second. Got derailed for a few moments looking for a theme that wasn't there. (I know, I know, the circles. But I always set those aside to think about later.)  

I liked that brief sidetrack as well as a few journeys similar to those Lewis mentioned. Jet Lee and Jet Set before Jet SKI. Like twisty dead-ends in corn mazes--another of my favorite things.

I'm always happy when I beat the average solve time on XWSTATS! Does anyone know if that stat uses the adjusted solve time? It seems like it would have to. There are definitely some days when I spend lots of time getting the last few squares... seems like if you were simply going for speed you might be able to get a faster solve time by just taking the penalty (although according to Google AI you get a 20% penalty when you use any hints [in addition to penalties for each time you use a hint], which sounds about right to me....)
"Thatssonotolay" too funny. Enjoyed today's puzzle. Thx

I’ve been behind for the past few days (finishing Thursday on Friday, Friday on Saturday, etc.) because on Wednesday, I started Jack Reuter’s absolutely amazing Sunday crossword from July 7, 2019. 

If you haven’t already completed it and you want a really fun challenge, I highly recommend it.  

Such was the complexity that Caitlin had to bring in a second expert to help write the column that day. Astoundingly (and I’d love to be proven wrong), it seems to be Jack Reuter’s one and only published crossword.
Flew through this one for new PR of 11:52! Didn't really understand the theme though
Virginia's state flag also has two unique sides. Just sayin'

The theme answers were very clever. I cottoned to the theme at 51-A, and went back to complete the first two themers. It was smooth sailing from there, though I wouldn't classify this puzzle as easy. It seemed like a perfectly conceived and leisurely Sunday solve. I clocked about 5 minutes under my average. 

I will seek out more of Mr. Steinberg's puzzles for sure.
One word. Genius.
I think this is the first Sunday I’ve solved without looking up a single thing! It took me an hour and 48 minutes, but it was fun the whole time.
This puzzle was great, I loved it! An alternate name for it would be "A little Caesar on the side"
Wow, this kid is sure gunning to take Shortz's job when he retires!
wonderful Sunday you gotta be a real Grinch if you're unable to enjoy this gem - it gets better upon reflection that's the mark of a classic puzzle bravo David

MISSED A BEAU

Sigh…When I was a young woman in Lisbon in the 1960s I went with an older man (40s) who opened up my world. His breadth of experience and wisdom made me feel like the chosen one, learning and loving from a true master of life. About 6 months into the relationship I spotted him at a cafe kissing another young woman. I ran up to them and started yelling, and they both laughed and brushed me away. I was completely devastated. When I saw him again I broke it off and he asked why. He didn’t understand the concept of monogamy. In my youthful naïveté I suppose I walked right into that one. It wasn’t that he had cheated, it was that I had foolishly believed our relationship was monogamous, when in reality we had never stipulated that. In his world that just didn’t exist. The moral of the story is never go to Lisbon in your 20s looking for love.

For some reason I thought the answer DISSONANTCHORES was DISSONANTSHORES because if you substituted a T for the SON you'd get DISTANTSHORES. I thought that was clever. 
I hung onto that, not knowing how it made any sense. It didn't and I surrendered after getting everything else.
Foiled by the crossers EMO(T/J)IPEDIA and (T/J)ERI.  It feels like an EMOTIPEDIA might be a thing.
Bravo bravo bravo!!! Chewy but a lot of fun -- and entirely solvable without having to look anything up. What a delight treat. More, please!!!!!!

Tootsies are toes, not feet. The singular is tootsie, I believe. Has anyone pointed this out? Am I wrong? Also, I don’t see how loading and unloading a dishwasher is dissonant. These are just 2 tiny quibbles about clueing, though. GREAT puzzle!
what happened to the answer key? Is it no longer available?
There's really nothing worse than a Sunday clued and filled like a Monday. Who gets enjoyment out of something like this?

Okay puzzle.  I thought the theme was a little boring, it took me till I was 3/4 of the way through to figure out the gimmick and when I did it was like "Oh, okay."

Some of the filler wasn't so good.  I assumed GRODY was youth slang but apparently it's 60 year old slang?  And crossing GUANACO, an animal I had never heard of.

I got stuck a while on the bottom right just because for a while I had BAND instead of CAST and it was giving crossings that didn't make sense.  And since so many of the filler around there was awkwardly phrased two word answers it took me a while to spot.

Today marks my 1500th solved NYT crossword. Note this is *not* consecutive; my current steak is 386. Still a milestone IMO. 

Of all those attempted since I started solving in 2022, I currently have four remaining unsolved, which I return to from time to time: One from 2008, two from 2023, and one from 2024. If and when I clear that list, I'll attempt more from the back catalog.

Thanks to Caitlin Lovinger for explaining the clever theme by David Steinberg. It almost passed me by! No word-checks but one lookup. I asked Siri just as I realized, “Oh, that’s a lookup.” Really enjoyed this puzzle. Thank you, David.

I thought it was funny that some circled letters changed (DISSONANT CHORES haha!) but I didn’t see that PLUS ONE meant the very next letter.
Nice to see another one from David Steinberg!
It was a pleasant enough puzzle for a lazy Sunday afternoon, but I had absolutely no idea what the theme was until I read this column.

I admire the skill and the concept. I just didn't really connect with this one. It happens. Some of the cluing seemed a bit overworked. 

I give the constructor a lot of credit for encouraging fills that seem inevitably correct at first but turn out to be wrong. 

Such as CAN I instead of COME for 83A. GIGA instead of the usual MEGA in front of WATT. And the time honored ADEN / OMAN  derailment. 

We now refer to a dishwasher that is full to the point of needing pre-natal care as being dishonant.

Meh. Unlike most I didn't find this puzzle satisfying or interesting. A little too gimmicky for me and some awkward clue phrasing and horrible puns. This is coming from someone who enjoys both Dad jokes and eye rolling puns. To each their own I suppose. At least it wasn't the dumpster fire from the day before.
David, go back to the drawing bored.
@Amy G Wow, very impressive!

@Amy G

That is fast!
@Amy G. Way to go!
@Amy G Way to go! I ended up exactly tying my Sunday PB 🙃

@Stocc Wow - 15'. Started puzzling 2 months ago. I am more a physicist and scientist, and retired. I was told that doing new stuff keeps your brain young. Owning neither TV, nor having social media, and not listening to music - these puzzles are hard. 6 hours 15' with cheating. Some times I look at the answer and am not even able to correlate clue and answer. Not sure how long I can stick with it - differential equations, general relativity and quantum mechanics are easier to understand - for me.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.

commentBody:
@Anita

😆😆😆
@Caitríona Shanahan dang! It seems like the emus didn’t like my clever (I thought) reply. Sigh 😔 . Look up the lyrics to the 1982 song “Valley Girl” by Frank Zappa, featuring Moon Unit Zappa.

@Cat Lady Margaret

"Gut course"?
I'd like to see you weave a casket!
Unless, of course, you mean that the casket has been made from the deceased's guts that have been woven together.
In which case, please remind me to stay away from that funeral home!

@Cat Lady Margaret
Excellent, CTM!

@CLM Glad to have scrolled down far enough to see your entries — always a joy when you post them. That first one immediately brought to mind this story: https://www.npr.org/2026/04/18/nx-s1-5786831/dying-terminally-ill-casket-weaving
@G: gosh! I didn't know about this. Now I feel churlish for making a joke about what is actually a beautiful art. Thanks for sharing!

@CLM

I don't think you should feel churlish at all! It's very cool that it's a real thing, and that your post reminded me of it so I could share. 🥰

@Marlene

Still, there's much more wheat than chaff here, as you see. Witty puns, grid analysis, personal vignettes, and even real controversy are discussed.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

Honest question:  Since streaks are irrelevant to you and on a given day you finish without the gold star, why do you flyspeck?  Why not just look at the finished grid whose link is available at the very bottom of wordplay to see where you erred and be done with it?

@Matt

That's a good question.  In the archives, where I frequently get hopelessly bogged down, I turn on Autocheck, get rid of the wrong ones, and try to finish it without any errors.

To me that's easier than googling, for all but the most googleable of googles.

I'm almost assured to finish the puzzle that way, as opposed to the uncertainty of flyspecking.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

commentBody:
@Matt

My pleasure, Doc.

@Andrzej

You were the one who inspired me to give up flyspecking (which even *sounds* unpleasant), and it’s been liberating. Once I freed myself from the tyranny of the gold star, I no longer had the all-too-frequent frustration of searching for that one fat-fingered letter.  

I know when I’ve finished a puzzle. I don’t need an app to praise me, any more than I need an emu to give me a scolding. (Note to other NYT games: you can keep your stinkin’ badges, too. What am I, a toddler?)

So come back, Andrezj, to the land where blue and gold stars twinkle together in harmony. It’s very relaxing here, and you can use that extra time to enjoy more important things, like scratching a fuzzy dog behind the ears.

@Heidi

I should not have mentioned the flyspecking. I don't usually engage in it any more. The other thing is much more important - I find easy Sundays pointless, and hard ones obnoxiously annoying. The bigger size of the grid makes both straightforward and challenging puzzles worse, IMO.

@Heidi

No worries, I was only mildy offended 🤣

I should elaborate on the flyspecking thing. What I meant was more complex than I managed to convey.

First, as a rule, I don't flyspeck. Typically, when I get the "so close" pop-up, I just check the puzzle, even on Tuesdays.

Second, I enjoy the occasional challenge and "clean solve". On some Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays, if I find the grid very hard but enjoyable (like yesterday), I push myself to get a gold star for a bit of extra satisfaction.

Now, I would never consider flyspecking a Sunday. It's simply unthinkable for me given the size of the grid. Thus, I find it impossible to solve and enjoy a hard Sunday as I might a challenging Saturday. The bigger size has no added value, for me. All it breeds is annoyance or boredom.

Here's a more thorough demonstration of how the sand art is created. This takes place in Nashville! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3WFOh0v1EzI

@lucky13
When I lived in Cleveland, the city hosted a creation of a Buddhist sand mandala, created on the marble floor of the atrium if City Hall. It took several weeks to create, and was open to the public. At the end, the monks swept the sand into a pile, and processed it down to Lake Erie, and dumped it in.

A perfect metaphor for the vanity and impermanence of existence. 

I should add that the process is not "big-C Creative," as the pattern of the mandala is entirely dictated by tradition

@lucky13
Thanks for putting us onto this video. I've seen videos like this before, and they never get old. Fascinating skill and patience.

@lucky13. Really looking forward to the upcoming Odyssey movie by the great Christopher Nolan!   Inspired by that, I tried my hand at an Odyssey-themed Sunday puzzle a while ago. But got rejected with the dreaded “ theme did not rise to the top”.  

Well, as Samuel Beckett (and Stan Wavrinka’s arm) said:  “Ever tried. Ever failed. No matter.  Try again. Fail again. Fail better.”

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Steve L

I am unable to escape her tractor beam.
I had never heard of EMOJIPEDIA, but that emoticons would have needed a -PEDIA never crossed my mind because they are ASCII characters.

@Barry Ancona
In various combinations. 
Much like words.
Which need 'pedias.

@PoloniusMonk it's not always the last letter that's wrong. In fact, for me it's almost never the last letter, but a mistake I made earlier.

The message is still frustrating, but only because I thought I hadn't made any mistakes.

@PoloniusMonk --- I hear you, but what I've learned is that you only get the message again if you delete (blank out) a square and type a new letter. If you type a new letter over an existing letter, and the puzzle is still not fully correct, you won't get the message again. You just know there's still work to do because you don't get the "Congratulations" happy music. 

Believe me this theory is well tested as I've typed over existing letters a zillion or so times over the years without getting the message again for still incorrect puzzles. Works for me in the browser, the Times app and the Games app (Android in my case). Hopefully it will work for you too!

@PoloniusMonk
What would be a better system / approach, to give solvers a chance to complete the puzzle? I don't fret over those, they just tell me I need to keep looking harder and put in the work. You could always just ask for the reveal. Any other system would feel like getting a "participation ribbon."

@Mark

When I first started doing NYT crosswords online, I was surprised that there was a "something's not quite right..." opportunity to fix an error.  Be it a fat-fingered typo, a wrong proper noun or any other kind of error, if one fills the final square and something's amiss, well it is a fail.  No gold star.  Students taking an exam don't get a little reprieve visit from the proctor saying "try again" so they can get their 100%.

How many of the streaks we read folks boasting about would have been dashed if wrong was wrong?

Flyspecking is an option, but it should be recognized as the cheat that it is.

@Don H
Wow. That's hard core. 
You never fatfinger an unintended error? You really think such is unforgivable and that the opportunity for finding and fixing such is "cheating"?

You do you but I'm glad you're not in charge.

(Also in rural Virginia despite the outdated locator.)

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@NYC Traveler
Ess-choo. Period. No ifs, ands, or buts. OED online shows Ess-choo as worldwide, with e-shoo in US usage only. Early usage also sometimes shows the prefix as EX.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@lucky13
Interesting. They must have found a line of pronunciation based on Italian cognate  /schiavone/, pronounced SKI-a-VO-nə.

@Neil horrifyingly long?? I got 38:38 today and was 9min faster than average.

Not sure what you’re comparing your time to, but seems like you might be being too harsh on yourself! I think you’re right on your first point, was easier for a Sunday; for that second point, if you were faster than your own average then there’s nothing “horrifying” about that time!

@Neil 

I'm approaching my 3000th New York Times crossword, but I can count the number of times I've finished a Sunday puzzle in less than an hour on one hand.

I spent a lifetime doing blue-collar jobs where the only metric my bosses cared about was speed. It was stressful. It made me dangerous when I was operating heavy machinery, it made me a menace on the road, and it made everyone around me cut corners in ways that never should have happened. Things got damaged and people got hurt.

The last thing I want to do is insert that mindset into something I do for fun.

@Neil
I'm still chuckling at your comment.

@jacob
Compare:
28:31, faster than average
28:31 faster than average

You replied to the former, but what was typed was the latter.
It may mean done in 1:00:00, average = 1:28:31.

@Bruce

Whenever I read that people are concerned about the time it takes to do a puzzle, I think of the word "pastime." That's what crosswords and other hobbies are in my opinion: things we do to pass the time!!
@Bruce 👏👏👏👏👏
Did you not deem the clued chores to be dissonant, or did you not recognize "dissonant chords" as a thing?

@Anonymous i don't think it's about the noises, I think it's supposed to be about the fact that it's two chores that are effectively incompatible because it would be difficult to do them at the same time. Like you, I didn't quite get it. That's not what "dissonant" means in a literal sense, and any good editor or English teacher would uncap a red pen upon seeing it used so loosely in a figurative sense. I'm willing to offer a bit of forgiveness to the constructor, because I can't imagine how difficult it would have been to come up with enough theme answers. Still, this one was absolutely a stretch in my book.
Nora, I posted the Wordplay link as a reply to your earlier comment. Here it is is again: https://archive.nytimes.com/wordplay.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/06/15/steam/

@Barry Ancona
Thanks Barry, that was a good column. And the comment that included a riff on Hamlet with “to Google or not to Google” was incredible. Made my day.

@Nora It is what it is… ;) Found the difficulty mostly on par with current Thursdays, though the last square was a Natick for me. Loved the trick once I finally (very late in the puzzle) figured it out!
@Mean Old Lady Merriam Webster disagrees with you on GRODY...

@HeathieJ

If I had to use the G word, I'd have spelt it GROTTY (which is how it sounds to me.)  It also sounds like a word someone made up because they had a limited vocabulary and couldn't come up with a word conveying the same meaning....

@Mean Old Lady,

“Grotty” is how I remember it when it *first* came into the lexicon, when the Beatles came to America and Beatlemania was in full swing.

Any kind of hip British slang was wildly popular, “grotty” was used to describe something gross.  Or possibly something cool. I don’t remember. 

Short for “grotesque”.

@Jerry
:-)
@Jerry -- Nice one, a SUS4 with an added 9th and 11th.  Thanks for bringing that up, haven't thought about it in a long time!

@Jerry I always considered music to be pretty straightforward – you play some notes and out comes a tune or symphony or something. After reading some of the comments on here about music theory, it turns out there's wasaay more to it than I thought. Terms for everything, most of which I've never heard of. You can learn a lot on this forum.

@Down_Home,

Your last sentence is my nomination for Understatement of the Year! 😄

@Jerry
I love me some sus chords too. Joni Mitchell called them " chords of inquiry".
But I don't think of them as parricularly 'dissonant'.

e.g. Herbie Hancock's Maiden Voyage

@Jerry
I had a music teacher once who had started out as a classical pianist turned jazz musician. He maintained that Beethoven was full of jazz chords that people didn't recognize because they were broken. He had me go through the Waldstein Sonata and circle and label all of them. Looking back at it now, I see I identified a heck of a lot of F-sharp diminished 7ths. I thought there were some 13ths in here but don't see them now.

@Jerry
You've inspired me to some research. I didn't identify suspended chords at the time, but looks as though some of these are.
@Jane Wheelaghan Thank you! I always wondered why Brits said "get the sack!"

@Frank Sartori, fewer look-ups means you're getting better at solving! 

Huzzah! 🎉🎉🎉

@Down_Home
Same

@Charles Peterson
Yep, ditto.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Grant
You didn't have to do anything of the sort. The villains at MLB are spewing my Jays games among Apple, Paramount+, and God knows what else. Result? I've cancelled my subscription to the cable sports station that was supposed to get me all their games and (wait for it!) listen on radio.
They've lost my cable fees, I still get to enjoy the games live, and I don't have to subscribe to four different systems.
Think outside the box.

@Kathleen880

The clanging of the pots, the clinking of the bowls, the jangling of the cutlery??

@McT
Only if you're very careless!  I seldom put a pot in the DW, and *never* bang it against anything.  Cutlery is carefully mixed so nothing gets "under-washed."  We use bone china (much lighter weight than, say, stoneware) and we're careful with it just as a general rule.  Back when the kids were growing up, we were careful with the stoneware.  I agree that doing the dishwasher load/unload is NOT a task that evokes thoughts of DISSONANCE.
@McT Oh, I was thinking that loading and unloading at the same time would be dissonant. But, I like yours better.

@McT well, yes, and no, I think. I didn’t think of the noisiness for a kind of comic reason: for some reason, my wife and I compete to see who can empty the dishwasher first, before the other one thinks of it. So when I’m unloading the dishwasher, I’m in a stealth mode, and can do it almost silently. So when I read the clanging and clinking idea, that made sense. However as a musician, I think of that chaos of sound as an arhythmic cacaphony, but not really a dissonance, since that requires pitched sounds, at least to a musician!

@Mean Old Lady

My unloads are silent, but Dear Husband will get up and unload the dishwasher in the middle of the night before I can get to it in the morning.     He clangs, clinks, and jangles away.  

I know, because the clangs, etc. wake me up.

He gets away with this because he bakes me brownies and cookies.   Really, really good brownies and cookies.

@Dr. OutreAmour,

I have absolutely no idea, but I like the animation at the end.

@Dr. OutreAmour It’s a play on 17A, a game show where contestants make “illusion cakes” and then are presented with things that may or may not be cakes. Winners (I suppose?) can fool others with their cakes while seeing through their fellow contestants’ illusions. To be clear, it’s not a great show, but watching it for 2 minutes or so is fun. And explanatory of the graphic in today’s midi.

@G
Yeah, I had the same reaction. A completely different kind of twisty!

@Dave Munger

Yes that stat uses the Adjusted Solve Time. I once checked a whole Sunday which made the adj. time 2+ hours, even though it’s only took 51 minutes wall clock time. The blurb said it was 1:30 slower than my best time meaning it had to use adj.
@J Thanks. I would love to see a stat for what portion of solvers used assistance (and what portion gave up). I think that might be a better stat for puzzle difficulty than solve time.
@J Do you happen to know how far back XW Stats goes in your solve history?

@Steve L Thank you. I knew it had to be a shorter time than from when I started doing the puzzle online, which was about 4 years ago (when I broke my shoulder and couldn't write for a while). The XWS average is always a lower time than my average from the NYT stats.
The only one in The Times, in any case. https://www.xwordinfo.com/Author/Jack_Reuter

@Josh
I worked it back then but did not get the gold star. Going to note this and come back to it if I ever retire. Thank you!

@Lynn It took me a couple days, so I wouldn’t have gotten a gold star even if I’d tackled it when it was new. 

Side note: Not to take anything away from the folks here with amazing crossword streaks, but this is part of my beef with streak culture in general: Sometimes there are good reasons to break a streak—like a unique and challenging crossword that deserves two days of puzzling. Streaking leads to diminished enjoyment of such challenges.

@Josh
I agree. I think it may be especially unhelpful during the first few years of solves.

@Josh
I went and looked. I solved with a gold star, but I am positive I did not understand the trick at the end. I looked at the comments and it was nice, but also sad,  to see comments from people who no longer or very infrequently appear here.
@CAC The state seal has two unique sides. Are you sure the flag does as well?
@Susan E astute observation

@Ace

This rings surprisingly familiar to an experience I also had in Lisbon in the ‘60s with a young American. She was staying in a flat off the rua das Flores with three other American friends. She loved Chopin and sangria. Is this you, Veronica? Has it been so long? How can I explain this enough times so that you understand? The woman at the cafe was Don Rickles in a Betty Buckley costume. We were rehearsing a skit that Don was to perform later that night at a charity event. I don’t know how I can make this any more clear. You broke my heart when you left, and I’ve never fully recovered.

@Ace
